articles.title: Federal Reserve Board's annual bank stress test confirms that large banks are well positioned to weather a severe recession and able to continue to lend to households and businesses
articles.raw_summary: Federal Reserve Board's annual bank stress test confirms that large banks are well positioned to weather a severe recession and able to continue to lend to households and businesses
articles.extracted_text: An official website of the United States Government Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you've safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. The Federal Reserve, the central bank of the United States, provides the nation with a safe, flexible, and stable monetary and financial system. The results of the Federal Reserve Board's annual bank stress test confirmed that large banks are well positioned to weather a severe recession and able to continue to lend to households and businesses. Despite absorbing more than $708 billion in total loan losses under this year's hypothetical scenario, capital declined only 1.6 percentage points in aggregate, staying above minimum capital requirements. "Today's results underscore the strength of the banking system," Vice Chair for Supervision Michelle W. Bowman said. "As we work to increase the transparency and accountability of the stress test, public feedback will help us continue to improve and instill greater confidence in the stress test and its results." As the Board previously announced , today's results will not impact large bank capital requirements, which have been published today. The current capital requirements will stay in place until 2027, when the stress test will be run with loss-estimating models that take public feedback into consideration. All 32 banks tested remained above their minimum common equity tier 1 capital requirements during this year's hypothetical recession scenario, which was similar in severity to the prior test. The hypothetical scenario this year included a severe global recession with a 39 percent decline in commercial real estate prices and a 30 percent decline in house prices. The unemployment rate also increased to a peak of 10 percent, and economic output declined commensurately. Three main factors influenced the results of this year's test, with two leading to a larger decline in the aggregate capital ratio than last year, and one more than offsetting this decline: The total projected losses include roughly $200 billion in credit card losses, $160 billion in losses from commercial and industrial loans, and $75 billion in losses from commercial real estate. For media inquiries, please email [email protected] or call 202-452-2955. 2026 Federal Reserve Stress Test Results (PDF) 2026 Large Bank Capital Requirements (PDF)

articles.title: ECB reports on progress towards euro adoption
articles.extracted_text: Limited progress has been made by the non-euro area Member States of the European Union (EU) on economic convergence with the euro area since 2024, according to the 2026 Convergence Report of the European Central Bank (ECB) published today. A key factor holding back economic convergence is the economic impact of external shocks, including Russia’s war against Ukraine, global trade tensions and the outbreak of the war in the Middle East. Despite these external challenges, economic activity in the countries under review has shown resilience, but the pace of growth has differed across countries. Looking ahead, the economic outlook is clouded by heightened geopolitical tensions. The war in the Middle East has contributed to greater volatility in global energy markets and rising energy costs, compounding the uncertainty about economic prospects. These developments have already had visible effects on inflation and economic sentiment, and may weigh on output growth in the future. While the degree of sensitivity and direct exposure of these economies to energy shocks has subsided over time and is currently lower than in 2022, the medium-term economic implications will depend on the intensity and duration of the present energy shock, as well as the scale of its indirect and second-round effects. As regards the price stability criterion , three of the five countries under review recorded a 12-month average inflation rate above the reference value of 2.7%. Inflation was considerably above the reference value in Romania and above it, albeit to a lesser extent, in Hungary and Poland. In the Czech Republic and Sweden, inflation was below the reference value (Chart 1). The reference value is based on the three best-performing Member States over the past 12 months, i.e. Cyprus (0.9%), France (1.2%) and Denmark (1.6%), taking their average inflation rates over the past 12 months and adding 1½ percentage points. None of the Member States was identified as an outlier for exclusion from the calculation of the reference value. General government surplus (+) or deficit (-) Source: Eurostat. Note: Data for 2023 have been revised slightly since the 2024 Convergence Report. The fiscal deficit has deteriorated in most countries since the 2024 Convergence Report, with debt-to-GDP ratios rising significantly in some cases. In 2025 Hungary, Poland and Romania exceeded the deficit reference value of 3% of GDP (Chart 2). The general government gross debt-to-GDP ratio in 2025 was below or well below the reference value of 60% in all of the countries under review except Hungary. However, the European Commission’s latest projections indicate that in 2026 the debt ratio will rise above the reference value in both Poland and Romania. The number of countries subject to an excessive deficit procedure has increased to three since the 2024 Convergence Report, with Hungary and Poland joining Romania – and none of these countries is projected to reduce its deficit to below the 3% of GDP reference value before the end of 2027, according to the European Commission’s projections. Romania continues to be subject to an excessive deficit procedure, which was opened in 2020 and for which the deadline for bringing the excessive deficit situation to an end has been extended to 2030. Excessive deficit procedures were also opened for Hungary and Poland in July 2024 based on deficits above the 3% of GDP reference value in 2023 – standing at 4.7% and 7.3% respectively – with deadlines for c

articles.title: Iceland joins TIPS for instant payments
articles.extracted_text: The European Central Bank (ECB) and Seðlabanki Íslands today signed an agreement for Iceland to join the Eurosystem’s TARGET Instant Payment Settlement ( TIPS ) system. This will allow payments in Icelandic króna to be settled instantly in central bank money as of 2028. The Icelandic króna will be the fifth currency available for settling retail payments instantly using the Eurosystem’s TARGET Services. The euro, Swedish krona and Danish krone are already available for settling payments in TIPS, while the Norwegian krone is scheduled to join in 2028. “We are delighted that more and more European countries, including from outside the European Union, are joining the Eurosystem’s TARGET Services,” said ECB President Christine Lagarde after signing the agreement. “This reinforces the integration of Europe’s financial infrastructures, supports its strategic autonomy, brings economies of scale and strengthens economic relations.” “This is an important step in modernising Iceland’s payments landscape and ensuring it is fit for the future,” said Seðlabanki Íslands’s Governor Ásgeir Jónsson. “Joining TIPS will strengthen both the security and efficiency of instant payments in Iceland. It will enhance the resilience of our payment infrastructure and bring our payment processes into line with SEPA practices, while enabling Iceland to benefit from the scale and expertise of the Eurosystem’s TARGET Services.” TARGET Services are developed and operated by the Eurosystem and rely on central bank money to facilitate transfers between banks, businesses and individuals. All TARGET Services are designed to be able to host transactions in currencies other than the euro. TIPS is the retail payment settlement system that enables banks and other payment service providers to offer their customers real-time fund transfers around the clock, every day of the year. articles.title: Boris Vujčić: Outlook for the euro area economy and monetary policy
Philip R. Lane: Introductory remarks
articles.extracted_text: It is a pleasure to be here for this timely exchange of views on the economic and fiscal implications for Europe of the situation in the Middle East. I will focus my remarks today on the implications for euro area economic activity and inflation, and for our monetary policy response. The peace agreement in the Middle East is welcome, but the situation remains fragile, with risks of setbacks or re-escalation. The full implications of the war for medium-term inflation and growth will depend on the intensity and duration of the energy price shock, and on the scale of its indirect and second-round effects. Let me start by reviewing the implications of the energy crisis for economic activity and the outlook from this point on. The euro area economy unexpectedly contracted in the first quarter of this year, by 0.2 per cent, due to a contraction in measured national activity in Ireland. Excluding Ireland, the euro area economy grew by 0.3 per cent, supported by domestic demand and exports. Public and private consumption contributed positively, while investment and inventories declined. However, the war in the Middle East is weighing on activity. Thus far, there has been a more pronounced weakening in services activity than in manufacturing; however, the support from precautionary inventory accumulation already seems to be fading, as new orders stagnated in May. The labour market remains resilient. The unemployment rate is still close to historical lows, standing at 6.3 per cent in April. Recent surveys point to some labour hoarding, meaning that firms are holding on to workers despite weaker business conditions. At the same time, labour demand has cooled further, and firms and households expect the labour market to weaken. Looking ahead, domestic demand is now projected to be weaker than was expected in March. The war is weighing on confidence and higher energy costs are reducing real incomes. Nonetheless, household balance sheets are solid overall, and consumption should remain the main driver of growth. Higher energy costs and lower confidence will be a drag on private investment in the short run, although it should be supported by firms investing in new digital technologies. In addition, higher government spending on defence and infrastructure should continue to support public investment. These factors are expected to provide some cushioning against the fallout from the war. The June Eurosystem staff baseline projections foresee real GDP growth of 0.8 per cent in 2026, 1.2 per cent in 2027 and 1.5 per cent in 2028. Let me now turn to the implications for inflation. Headline inflation increased to 3.2 per cent in May, from 3.0 per cent in April. Although energy prices declined compared with the previous month, base effects meant that annual energy inflation remained at 10.8 per cent, unchanged from April. Non-energy inflation increased by 0.2 percentage points, to 2.4 per cent. Within this, food inflation decreased to 1.9 per cent from 2.4 per cent, while core inflation increased to 2.6 per cent from 2.2 per cent. Goods inflation inched up by 0.1 percentage points, to 0.9 per cent, and services inflation rose by 0.5 percentage points, to 3.5 per cent. Domestic cost pressures eased in the first quarter, on the back of slower growth in wages and profits. Profit margins continued to shrink, indicating that profits continued to absorb part of the impact of higher labour costs. The energy shock is not yet feeding into wages. Annual growth in nego

articles.title: 2.Industrial Production Operation in May 2026
articles.extracted_text: In May, the total value added of industrial enterprises above the designated size increased by 4.5% year on year in real terms (the growth rates of value added are the real growth rates after deducting price factors), 0.4 percentage points higher than the previous month. On a month-on-month basis, in May, the total value added of industrial enterprises above the designated size increased by 0.40% over the previous month. From January to May, the total value added of industrial enterprises above the designated size increased by 5.4% year on year. In terms of three sectors, in May, the value added of the mining industry increased by 2.3% year on year, that of the manufacturing industry increased by 4.4%, and that of the production and supply of electricity, heat power, gas and water increased by 7.6%. In terms of economic types, in May, the value added of state-holding enterprises increased by 3.7% year on year; that of share-holding enterprises increased by 5.2%; that of enterprises funded by foreign investors and investors from Hong Kong, Macao, and Taiwan increased by 1.9%; and that of private enterprises increased by 2.7%. In terms of industries, in May, the value added of 28 out of 41 major industries continued to show year-on-year growth. Among them, the value added of the mining and washing of coal went up by 3.5%, that of the extraction of petroleum and natural gas up by 1.5%, that of the processing of food from agricultural and sideline products up by 1.5%, that of the manufacture of liquor, beverages and refined tea down by 2.7%, that of the textile industry up by 2.6%, that of the manufacture of raw chemical materials and chemical products up by 0.3%, that of the manufacture of non-metallic mineral products down by 5.6%, that of the smelting and pressing of ferrous metals up by 1.6%, that of the smelting and pressing of non-ferrous metals down by 4.5%, that of the manufacture of general-purpose machinery up by 6.7%, that of the manufacture of special-purpose machinery up by 9.1%, that of the manufacture of automobiles up by 8.3%, that of the manufacture of railway, ship, aerospace and other transport equipment up by 7.4%, that of the manufacture of electrical machinery and apparatus up by 4.7%, that of the manufacture of computers, communication equipment and other electronic equipment up by 17.0%, and that of the production and supply of electricity and heat power up by 8.7%. In terms of products, in May, the output of 300 out of 626 products produced by industrial enterprises above the designated size increased year on year. Among them, the output of rolled steel was 123.03 million tons, down by 2.8% year on year; that of cement was 149.91 million tons, down by 8.1%; that of ten kinds of non-ferrous metals was 6.98 million tons, up by 2.2%; that of ethylene was 3.38 million tons, up by 2.1%; that of motor vehicles was 2.582 million sets, down by 3.2%, including 1.489 million new energy vehicles, up by 17.8%; the electricity generation was 784.3 billion kWh, up by 4.2%; and the crude oil processing volume was 53.72 million tons, down by 9.1%. In May, the product sales ratio of industrial enterprises above the designated size was 96.0%, a year-on-year decrease of 0.1 percentage points; and the export delivery value realized by industrial enterprises above the designated size was 1,388.4 billion yuan, a nominal increase of 10.1% year on year. articles.title: 3.Investment in Fixed Assets from January to May 2026
articles.extracted_text: From January to May 2026, the national investment in fixed assets (excluding rural households) was 17,851.2 billion yuan, a year-on-year decrease of 4.1% (calculated on a comparable basis, see Annotation 7 for details). Specifically, the investment in intellectual property products increased by 9.3% year on year. By industry, the investment in the primary industry was 388.7 billion yuan, a year-on-year increase of 5.9%; the investment in the secondary industry was 6,498.9 billion yuan, up 0.1%; and the investment in the tertiary industry was 10,963.6 billion yuan, down 6.8%. The industrial investment increased by 0.1% year on year. Specifically, the investment in mining increased by 6.2%, the investment in manufacturing decreased by 0.4%, and the investment in production and supply of electricity, heat power, gas and water decreased by 0.1%. The investment in infrastructure (see Annotation 1 for details of statistical scope) increased by 0.6% year on year. Specifically, the investment in waterway transportation increased by 23.3%, the investment in civil aviation transportation increased by 21.7%, the investment in ecological conservation and environmental governance increased by 3.5%, and the investment in the production and supply of gas increased by 2.8%. By region, the investment in the eastern region decreased by 6.4% year on year, the investment in the central region decreased by 2.9%, the investment in the western region decreased by 6.6%, and the investment in the northeastern region decreased by 17.5%. By statistical category of registration, the investment in fixed assets of domestic-invested enterprises decreased by 3.9% year on year, the investment in fixed assets of enterprises funded by investors from Hong Kong, Macao, and Taiwan decreased by 8.7%, and the investment in fixed assets of foreign-invested enterprises decreased by 4.3%. From January to May 2026, non-governmental investment in fixed assets decreased by 7.1% year on year. On a month-on-month basis, the investment in fixed assets (excluding rural households) decreased by 1.91% in May. articles.title: 4.Investment in Real Estate Development from January to May 2026
articles.extracted_text: I. The Completion of Investment in Real Estate Development From January to May, the investment in real estate development was 3,035.6 billion yuan, a year-on-year decrease of 16.2% (calculated on a comparable basis, see Annotation 5 for details), of which the investment in residential buildings was 2,342.6 billion yuan, down 15.6%. From January to May, the floor space of buildings under construction by real estate development enterprises was 5,487.75 million square meters, a year-on-year decrease of 12.3%. Specifically, the floor space of residential buildings under construction was 3,808.30 million square meters, down 12.6%. The floor space of buildings newly started was 179.29 million square meters, down 22.6%. Specifically, the floor space of residential buildings newly started was 130.84 million square meters, down 23.4%. The floor space of buildings completed was 140.87 million square meters, down 23.4%. Specifically, the floor space of residential buildings completed was 99.99 million square meters, down 25.0%. II. Sales of Newly Built Commercial Buildings From January to May, the floor space of newly built commercial buildings sold was 313.20 million square meters, a year-on-year decrease of 10.8%, of which the floor space of residential buildings sold decreased by 12.1%. The sales of newly built commercial buildings were 2,936.6 billion yuan, down 13.5%, of which the sales of residential buildings decreased by 14.1%. At the end of May, the floor space of commercial buildings for sale was 771.82 million square meters, down 0.4% year on year. Specifically, the floor space for sale held for less than three years was 571.52 million square meters, down 2.8%. III. Funds for Investment This Year for Real Estate Development Enterprises From January to May, the funds for investment this year for real estate development enterprises were 3,275.6 billion yuan, a year-on-year decrease of 19.0%. Among them, domestic loans were 487.5 billion yuan, down 28.7%; self-raised funds were 1,198.5 billion yuan, down 13.0%; deposits and advance receipts were 1,001.2 billion yuan, down 16.1%; individual mortgage loans were 406.6 billion yuan, down 28.0%. articles.title: 5.Total Retail Sales of Consumer Goods from January to May 2026
articles.extracted_text: From January to May, the total retail sales of consumer goods reached 20,603.1 billion yuan, up by 1.4% year on year. Specifically, the retail sales of consumer goods excluding automobiles reached 19,002.2 billion yuan, up by 2.7%. In May, the total retail sales of consumer goods reached 4,109.0 billion yuan, down by 0.6% year on year. Specifically, the retail sales of consumer goods excluding automobiles reached 3,778.1 billion yuan, up by 1.1%. By business area, from January to May, the retail sales of consumer goods in urban areas reached 17,866.2 billion yuan, up by 1.2% year on year; those in rural areas were 2,736.9 billion yuan, up by 2.6%. In May, the retail sales of consumer goods in urban areas reached 3,574.1 billion yuan, down by 0.9% year on year; those in rural areas were 534.9 billion yuan, up by 1.5%. By consumption pattern, from January to May, the retail sales of goods were 18,254.3 billion yuan, up by 1.2% year on year; the income of the catering industry was 2,348.8 billion yuan, up by 3.1%. In May, the retail sales of goods were 3,648.5 billion yuan, down by 0.7% year on year; the income of the catering industry was 460.5 billion yuan, up by 0.6%. By retail format, from January to May, the retail sales of convenience stores and supermarkets among retail enterprises (units) above the designated size increased by 6.8% and 3.6% year on year, respectively; those of specialty stores, department stores and brand-exclusive stores decreased by 1.2%, 1.8% and 7.6%, respectively. From January to May, Chinaâs online retail sales of goods and services reached 8,317.7 billion yuan, up by 5.9% year on year. Specifically, the online retail sales of goods were 5,271.8 billion yuan, up by 5.0%; among the online retail sales of goods, those of food, clothing, and daily necessities increased by 15.5%, 7.2%, and 1.6%, respectively. The online retail sales of services were 3,045.9 billion yuan, up by 7.6%. articles.title: 6.Energy Production in May 2026
articles.extracted_text: In May 2026, the raw coal production by industrial enterprises above the designated size remained at a relatively high level, the growth of crude oil production was steady, the growth of natural gas production fell slightly, and the growth of electricity generation accelerated. I. Raw Coal, Crude Oil and Natural Gas Production and Related Information Raw coal production remained at a relatively high level. In May, the raw coal production by industrial enterprises above the designated size was 400 million tons, down by 1.7% year on year; the average daily output was 12.81 million tons. From January to May, the raw coal production by industrial enterprises above the designated size was 1.98 billion tons, a year-on-year decrease of 0.3%. The growth of crude oil production was steady. In May, the crude oil production by industrial enterprises above the designated size was 18.57 million tons, a year-on-year increase of 0.5%, 0.7 percentage points lower than that in April; the average daily output was 599 thousand tons. From January to May, the crude oil production by industrial enterprises above the designated size was 91.31 million tons, a year-on-year increase of 1.1%. The decline in the processing volume of crude oil expanded. In May, the processing volume of crude oil by industrial enterprises above the designated size was 53.72 million tons, a year-on-year decrease of 9.1%, with the decline expanded by 3.3 percentage points from April; the average daily processing volume was 1.733 million tons. From January to May, the processing volume of crude oil by industrial enterprises above the designated size was 292.80 million tons, a year-on-year decrease of 2.2%. Natural gas production fell slightly. In May, the production of natural gas by industrial enterprises above the designated size was 21.7 billion cubic meters, a year-on-year decrease of 2.2%, compared with a 1.9% increase in April; the average daily output was 700 million cubic meters. From January to May, the production of natural gas by industrial enterprises above the designated size was 111.7 billion cubic meters, a year-on-year increase of 1.7%. Electricity generation by industrial enterprises above the designated size grew faster. In May, electricity generation by industrial enterprises above the designated size was 784.3 billion kWh, a year-on-year increase of 4.2%, with the growth rate 1.6 percentage points higher than that in April; the average daily electricity generation was 25.30 billion kWh. From January to May, electricity generation by industrial enterprises above the designated size was 3,912.9 billion kWh, a year-on-year increase of 3.6%. By category, in May, the growth of thermal power generation by industrial enterprises above the designated size slowed down, while that of hydropower and solar power generation accelerated; the growth of nuclear power and wind power generation turned from decline to growth. Among them, thermal power generation by industrial enterprises above the designated size increased by 2.1% year on year, with the growth rate 1.0 percentage point lower than that in April; hydropower generation increased by 13.0%, with the growth rate 0.8 percentage points higher than that in April; nuclear power generation increased by 5.0%, compared with an 8.7% decline in April; wind power generation increased by 0.5%, compared with a 5.0% decline in April; solar power generation increased by 12.1%, with the growth rate 5.0 percentage points higher than that i

articles.title: 7.National Economy Showed Steady Momentum with Innovation-led and High-quality Developmen...
articles.extracted_text: In May, under the strong leadership of the Central Committee of the Communist Party of China (CPC) with Comrade Xi Jinping at its core, all regions and departments fully implemented the decisions and arrangements made by the CPC Central Committee and the State Council, adhered to the general principle of pursuing progress while ensuring stability, fully and faithfully applied the new development philosophy on all fronts, moved faster to foster a new pattern of development, strictly implemented more proactive and effective macro policies, and effectively addressed external shocks and challenges. As a result, production and supply registered steady progress, employment and prices were generally stable, foreign trade demonstrated strong resilience, and new growth drivers witnessed robust development. The national economy maintained steady momentum with innovation-led and high-quality development. 1. Industrial Production Witnessed Accelerated Growth and Equipment Manufacturing and High-Tech Manufacturing Grew Fast. In May, the total value added of industrial enterprises above the designated size grew by 4.5 percent year on year, 0.4 percentage points faster than that of the previous month; the month-on-month growth was 0.40 percent. In terms of sectors, the value added of mining increased by 2.3 percent year on year, that of manufacturing grew by 4.4 percent, and that of production and supply of electricity, heat power, gas and water went up by 7.6 percent. The value added of equipment manufacturing increased by 9.5 percent year on year and that of high-tech manufacturing increased by 15.1 percent, 1.2 percentage points and 2.3 percentage points faster than that of the previous month respectively. An analysis by types of ownership showed that the value added of state holding enterprises was up by 3.7 percent year on year; that of share-holding enterprises was up by 5.2 percent; that of enterprises funded by foreign investors or investors from Hong Kong, Macao and Taiwan was up by 1.9 percent; and that of private enterprises was up by 2.7 percent. In terms of products, the production of 3D printing devices, lithium-ion batteries and industrial robots grew by 54.4 percent, 40.0 percent and 27.9 percent year on year respectively. In the first five months, the value added of industrial enterprises above the designated size went up by 5.4 percent year on year. In May, the Manufacturing Purchasing Managersâ Index stood at 50.0 percent. The Production and Operation Expectation Index was 53.9 percent. In the first four months, the total profits made by industrial enterprises above the designated size were 2,435.8 billion yuan, up by 18.2 percent year on year. 2. Service Sector Grew Steadily and Modern Services Showed Good Momentum of Development. In May, the Index of Services Production grew by 4.4 percent year on year, 0.1 percentage points faster than that of the previous month. Specifically, that of information transmission, software and information technology services, leasing and business services, finance, and transport, storage and postal services grew by 11.3 percent, 10.9 percent, 7.0 percent and 4.8 percent year on year respectively. In the first five months, the Index of Services Production increased by 4.8 percent year on year. In the first four months, the business revenue of service enterprises above the designated size grew by 6.4 percent year on year. In May, the Business Activity Index for Services stood at 50.3 percent and the

articles.title: 8.Sales Prices of Commercial Residential Buildings in 70 Medium and Large-sized Cities in...
articles.extracted_text: Sales Price Indices of Newly Constructed Commercial Residential Buildings in 70 Large and Medium-Sized Cities Sales Price Indices of Second-Hand Residential Buildings in 70 Large and Medium-Sized Cities Price Indices of Newly Constructed Commercial Residential Buildings by Floor Space in 70 Large and Medium-Sized Cities Price Indices of Second-Hand Residential Buildings by Floor Space in 70 Large and Medium-Sized Cities 1. Survey Coverage: The survey was conducted in the municipal districts of 70 medium and large-sized cities, excluding the counties. 2. Survey Methods: The survey of newly constructed commercial residential buildingsâ sales prices is an overall survey, with basic data derived from online contract-signing data provided by local real estate management departments. The survey of second-hand residential buildingsâ sales prices combines the survey of main units with typical survey, with basic data sourced from reports submitted by real estate agencies or housing service platforms, as well as on-site price collection by surveyors. 3. The calculation of the price indices can be seen in the Statistical Reporting System on Real Estate Prices. 4. If there is no deal record this month, the overall price level is regarded as unchanged. 5. According to the requirements of the statistical system, starting from January 2026, the statistical survey of real estate prices in 70 medium and large-sized cities takes 2025 as the base period for a new round of comparison. The weights of basic categories for each city have been adjusted to better reflect changes in the residential building sales structure. According to the calculation, the impact of the base period rotation on the monthly year-on-year sales price indices of commercial residential buildings in 70 medium and large-sized cities is within 0.03 percentage points on average.

articles.title: 9.Market Prices of Important Means of Production in Circulation, June 1-10, 2026
articles.extracted_text: China Economic Monitoring and Analysis Center In early June 2026, according to the monitoring of market prices of 50 kinds of important means of production under 9 categories in the national circulation field, the prices of 14 products increased, 33 kinds decreased, and 3 kinds remained flat compared with those in late May 2026. Market prices of important means of production in circulation refer to the wholesale prices and selling prices of enterprises trading important means of production. Different from producer prices, market prices of means of production include producer prices, as well as circulation expenses, profits, and taxes of the enterprises. Producer prices and market prices influence each other. Due to a time lag, their movements may show divergence in a certain period of time. The monitoring of market prices of important means of production in circulation includes the prices of 50 kinds of products under 9 categories. For details of product categories and specifications, please see the attached table. The monitoring covers nearly 2,000 wholesalers, agents, and dealers of more than 300 trading markets in 31 provinces (autonomous regions and municipalities). Price monitoring methods include information officers gathering prices on-site or requesting prices by telephone, instant messaging tools, e-mail, and so on. The number of products with price increases, decreases, and no change is counted based on the percentage change (%). articles.title: 10.Consumer Price Index in May 2026
articles.extracted_text: In May 2026, Chinaâs Consumer Price Index (CPI) increased by 1.2% year on year. Specifically, the price index in urban areas increased by 1.3% while that in rural areas increased by 1.1%; the price index for food decreased by 1.7% while that for non-food increased by 1.9%; the price index for consumer goods increased by 1.6% while that for services increased by 0.8%. From January to May, on average, Chinaâs CPI increased by 1.0% year on year. In May, Chinaâs CPI decreased by 0.1% month on month. Specifically, the price index in urban areas decreased by 0.1% while that in rural areas decreased by 0.1%; the price index for food decreased by 0.4% while that for non-food decreased by 0.1%; the price index for consumer goods decreased by 0.2% while that for services decreased by 0.1%. I. Year-on-Year Changes in Price Indexes for Various Goods and Services In May, the price index for food, tobacco, liquor and dining out decreased by 0.9% year on year, bringing the CPI down by about 0.26 percentage points. Among food, the price index for meat of livestock decreased by 7.4%, bringing the CPI down by about 0.31 percentage points, of which the price index for pork decreased by 16.1%, bringing the CPI down by about 0.31 percentage points; that for fresh fruits decreased by 2.2%, bringing the CPI down by about 0.04 percentage points; that for eggs increased by 6.6%, pushing the CPI up by about 0.03 percentage points; that for fresh vegetables increased by 1.6%, pushing the CPI up by about 0.02 percentage points; that for aquatic products increased by 0.6%, pushing the CPI up by about 0.01 percentage points. Among the price indexes for the other seven major categories, six increased, and one decreased year on year. Specifically, the price indexes for miscellaneous goods and services, transportation and telecommunication, and health care and medical services increased by 9.9%, 5.4% and 2.1%, respectively; the price indexes for household facilities, articles and services, clothing and education, culture and recreation increased by 1.8%, 1.4% and 1.3%, respectively; the price index for residence decreased by 0.2%. II. Month-on-Month Changes in Price Indexes for Various Goods and Services In May, the price index for food, tobacco, liquor and dining out decreased by 0.2% month on month, bringing the CPI down by about 0.06 percentage points. Among food, the price index for fresh vegetables decreased by 3.6%, bringing the CPI down by about 0.06 percentage points; that for meat of livestock decreased by 0.8%, bringing the CPI down by about 0.03 percentage points, of which the price index for pork decreased by 1.6%, bringing the CPI down by about 0.03 percentage points; that for eggs increased by 5.0%, pushing the CPI up by about 0.03 percentage points; and that for fresh fruits increased by 0.6%, pushing the CPI up by about 0.01 percentage points. Among the price indexes for the other seven major categories, one increased, four decreased and two remained flat month on month. Specifically, the price index for clothing increased by 0.6%; the price indexes for education, culture and recreation, and health care and medical services remained flat; the price indexes for household facilities, articles and services and miscellaneous goods and services both decreased by 0.4%; the price indexes for transportation and telecommunication and residence decreased by 0.3% and 0.1%, respectively. IV. Household facilities, articles and services Use and maintenance of t

articles.title: 11.Industrial Producer Price Indexes in May 2026
articles.extracted_text: In May 2026, Chinaâs producer price index for industrial products (PPI) increased by 3.9% year on year and 0.5% month on month. The purchasing price index for industrial producers increased by 5.8% year on year and 1.3% month on month. From January to May, on average, the producer price index for industrial products increased by 1.0% year on year, and the purchasing price index for industrial producers increased by 1.6%. I. Year-on-Year Changes in Industrial Producer Price Indexes In May, among producer price indexes for industrial products, the price index for means of production increased by 5.2% year on year, pushing the overall producer price index for industrial products up by about 4.08 percentage points. Specifically, the price index for mining and quarrying increased by 15.8%, that for raw materials increased by 9.2%, and that for the processing industry increased by 2.3%. The price index for consumer goods decreased by 0.8%, bringing the overall producer price index for industrial products down by about 0.18 percentage points. Specifically, the price index for food decreased by 1.8%, that for clothing and articles for daily use both decreased by 1.0%, and that for durable consumer goods remained flat. Among purchasing price indexes for industrial producers, the price index for nonferrous metals and cables increased by 22.0%, that for raw chemical materials increased by 11.8%, that for fuel and power increased by 10.0%, that for textile materials increased by 2.5%, that for ferrous metals increased by 0.3%, that for building materials and non-metals decreased by 5.5%, and that for agricultural and sideline products decreased by 1.6%. II. Month-on-Month Changes in Industrial Producer Price Indexes In May, among producer price indexes for industrial products, the price index for means of production increased by 0.7% month on month, pushing the overall producer price index for industrial products up by about 0.53 percentage points. Specifically, the price index for mining and quarrying increased by 1.5%, that for raw materials increased by 0.9%, and that for the processing industry increased by 0.5%. The price index for consumer goods remained flat. Specifically, the price index for food decreased by 0.1%, and that for clothing, articles for daily use and durable consumer goods all increased by 0.1%. Among purchasing price indexes for industrial producers, the price index for raw chemical materials increased by 4.2%, that for fuel and power increased by 2.7%, that for textile materials increased by 1.0%, that for ferrous metals increased by 0.6%, that for nonferrous metals and cables and for agricultural and sideline products both increased by 0.3%, and that for building materials and non-metals both decreased by 0.5%. articles.title: Meeting on the development of the domestic aviation industry
articles.raw_summary: The President held a meeting on the development of the domestic aviation industry at the Gromov Flight Research Institute.
articles.extracted_text: All content on this site is licensed under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International The President held a meeting on the development of the domestic aviation industry at the Gromov Flight Research Institute. The meeting was attended by First Deputy Prime Minister Denis Manturov Manturov Denis First Deputy Prime Minister of the Government of the Russian Federation , Deputy Prime Minister Vitaly Savelyev Savelyev Vitaly Deputy Prime Minister of the Government of the Russian Federation , Deputy Prime Minister – Presidential Plenipotentiary Envoy to the Far Eastern Federal District Yury Trutnev Trutnev Yury Deputy Prime Minister of the Government of the Russian Federation – Plenipotentiary Presidential Envoy to the Far Eastern Federal District , Deputy Chief of Staff of the Presidential Executive Office Maxim Oreshkin Oreshkin Maxim Deputy Chief of Staff of the Presidential Executive Office , Minister of Industry and Trade Anton Alikhanov Alikhanov Anton Minister of Industry and Trade of the Russian Federation , Minister of Transport Andrei Nikitin Nikitin Andrei Minister of Transport , Minister of Economic Development Maxim Reshetnikov Reshetnikov Maxim Minister of Economic Development of the Russian Federation , Finance Minister Anton Siluanov Siluanov Anton Minister of Finance of the Russian Federation , Central Bank Governor Elvira Nabiullina Nabiullina Elvira Governor of the Central Bank of the Russian Federation (Bank of Russia) , Head of the Republic of Tatarstan Rustam Minnikhanov Minnikhanov Rustam Head of the Republic of Tatarstan , Head of the Federal Air Transport Agency Dmitry Yadrov, Chairman of the Management Board and CEO of Aeroflot – Russian Airlines Sergei Alexandrovsky, Scientific Director of the National Research Centre Zhukovsky Institute Boris Alyoshin, CEO of United Aircraft Corporation Vadim Badekha, President and Chairman of the Management Board of Sberbank German Gref Gref German Chairman of the Management Board and CEO of Sberbank , CEO of the National Research Centre Zhukovsky Institute Andrei Dutov, S7 Group shareholder Vladislav Filyov, Rostec CEO Sergei Chemezov Chemezov Sergei CEO of Rostec State Corporation , and Chairman of the State Development Corporation VEB.RF Igor Shuvalov Shuvalov Igor Chairman of the State Development Corporation VEB.RF . President of Russia Vladimir Putin: Colleagues, good afternoon. Today’s agenda covers civil aviation development. We will discuss the air transport sector and the aircraft manufacturing. I would like to emphasise once again that, for Russia, the aviation industry is, as we all understand, of particular importance. The state of this sector directly affects transport connectivity of the regions across our vast country, and the speed and convenience of travel. What is crucial is the ability to independently produce the full range of military and civilian aircraft. This is a key indicator of the country’s technological, scientific and industrial sovereignty. Indeed, just a few countries possess such unique competencies, and Russia is one of them. My colleagues have just made the point that even highly developed nations – those with long-standing traditions and impressive aircraft manufacturing records – continue to benefit from broad international collaboration. For a variety of reasons, chiefly sanctions pressure, the landscape here has shifted. Russia was compelled to pursue a policy of full import substitution, and has demonstrably risen to that challenge. More

articles.title: Visit to Gromov Flight Research Institute
articles.raw_summary: Vladimir Putin visited the Gromov Flight Research Institute in Zhukovsky, Moscow Region.
articles.extracted_text: All content on this site is licensed under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International Vladimir Putin visited the Gromov Flight Research Institute in Zhukovsky, Moscow Region. The President was presented with advanced models of Russian aircraft, including the next-generation Yakovlev MC-21–310 medium-range passenger aircraft powered by the PD-14 engine, the Sukhoi SJ-100 equipped with the PD-8 engine, and the Ilyushin Il-114–300 turboprop aircraft. Accompanying the head of state were First Deputy Prime Minister Denis Manturov Manturov Denis First Deputy Prime Minister of the Government of the Russian Federation , Deputy Prime Minister Vitaly Savelyev Savelyev Vitaly Deputy Prime Minister of the Government of the Russian Federation , Minister of Industry and Trade Anton Alikhanov Alikhanov Anton Minister of Industry and Trade of the Russian Federation , Minister of Finance Anton Siluanov Siluanov Anton Minister of Finance of the Russian Federation , Rostec CEO Sergei Chemezov Chemezov Sergei CEO of Rostec State Corporation , President and Chairman of the Management Board of Sberbank of Russia German Gref Gref German Chairman of the Management Board and CEO of Sberbank , Head of the Federal Air Transport Agency Dmitry Yadrov, and CEO of PJSC United Aircraft Corporation Vadim Badekha. Explanations were provided by the chief designers and test pilots. The Gromov Flight Research Institute is the leading enterprise in the Russian aviation industry specialising in the comprehensive research and testing of aircraft and onboard systems.

articles.title: Greetings to the 3rd National Children’s Folk Art Festival
articles.raw_summary: Vladimir Putin sent greetings to the organisers, participants and guests of the 3 rd National Children’s Folk Art Festival which is taking place as part of the Year of Unity of the Peoples of Russia.
articles.extracted_text: All content on this site is licensed under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International Vladimir Putin sent greetings to the organisers, participants and guests of the 3 rd National Children’s Folk Art Festival which is taking place as part of the Year of Unity of the Peoples of Russia. “It is symbolic that it was here, in Bashkortostan, that the idea of holding this marvellous celebration of friendship and creative networking was born. Today, the capital of this multi-ethnic, hospitable republic is welcoming more than a thousand young talents from across our vast country. Let me emphasise that we sincerely treasure our rich historical, cultural and spiritual legacy, with a focus on preserving the unique traditions and customs of the peoples inhabiting Russia. Their vibrant and unique art makes us proud. I am very pleased to note therefore that your wonderful project has been so popular among young people and has been attracting more participants every year. I am convinced that the 3 rd National Children’s Folk Art Festival will be a great success, offering its spectators a chance to enjoy performances by folk, dance and choir ensembles, leaving unforgettable impressions. Of course, it must help promote the values of our multi-ethnic culture among young people.”

articles.title: Presenting the Order of Zhukov to the Presidential Regiment
articles.raw_summary: Vladimir Putin presented the Order of Zhukov to the Presidential Regiment. The ceremony took place in the St George Hall of the Grand Kremlin Palace. The regiment marks its 90 th anniversary in 2026.
articles.extracted_text: All content on this site is licensed under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International Vladimir Putin presented the Order of Zhukov to the Presidential Regiment. The ceremony took place in the St George Hall of the Grand Kremlin Palace. The regiment marks its 90 th anniversary in 2026. President of Russia Vladimir Putin : Comrades officers, warrant officers, sergeants, soldiers, This year, the Presidential Regiment of the Moscow Kremlin’s Commandant’s Office within the Federal Guard Service of Russia marks its 90 th anniversary. I would like to convey my heartfelt greetings to the regiment’s veterans and all its personnel on this marvellous occasion. We have gathered in the Moscow Kremlin’s St George Hall, which serves as one of the main symbols and helps preserve the memory of the Fatherland’s military valour, the legendary battles and heroes of 1812, conveying a direct and unbreakable bond and continuity in the country’s military history. Since its creation in 1936, the regiment’s personnel have distinguished themselves through excellent military and special training, and have always fulfilled their objectives, while serving as role models for military personnel from other units and formations. The regiment’s soldiers and officers served well during the harsh years of the Great Patriotic War and demonstrated their professionalism in military affairs, as well as their best qualities and love for their Fatherland. The regiment received the Wartime Red Banner for its unparalleled bravery, fidelity to its duty and oath in 1944, and in the summer of 1945, a mixed battalion of the Kremlin regiment took part in the legendary Victory Parade. It joined the frontline heroes in marching across the country’s main square, which served as a symbol of the strength and greatness of our nation and state. It is heartening that you have been preserving and building on the invaluable traditions of your predecessors in today’s world while honouring the memory of your fellow servicemen. The names of 99 officers and soldiers who died valiantly while fighting Nazism will remain forever on the regiment’s lists. I would like to place a special emphasis on the fact that you are constantly improving your military skills by mastering the latest technology and weapons, while serving as role models in terms of your bearing and appearance. It is symbolic that you have been entrusted with defending the Moscow Kremlin, the heart of Russia, as well as many other major state facilities, performing responsible ceremonial functions, including by serving in the honour guard of the Eternal Flame at the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier, and taking part in the changing of the unmounted and mounted guard on the Kremlin’s Cathedral Square. I would like to thank you for your service, for your determination and your loyalty to your oath, for the courage you displayed during times of trial, when the fate of Russia was at stake. We know that you will never let us down and will continue to fulfil the tasks assigned to you with honour. Once again, I would like to congratulate the regiment’s personnel on this anniversary. I wish you good health, new achievements, wellbeing and all the best to your loved ones. We can now proceed with our honourable mission and procedure. Vladimir Putin : Comrades, congratulations on receiving a state decoration. Hooray! Commander of the Presidential Regiment Major General Mikhail Suraikin : Comrade President of the Russian Federation. Allow me to express

articles.title: Meeting with Government members
articles.raw_summary: The President held a videoconference meeting with Government members.
articles.extracted_text: All content on this site is licensed under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International The President held a videoconference meeting with Government members. The meeting focused on the launch of a mentoring programme for medical school graduates. Several current issues were also addressed at the beginning of the meeting. President of Russia Vladimir Putin: Good afternoon, colleagues, Today, our agenda includes a number of important economic and social issues including the development of the human resources potential of the domestic healthcare system. Last Sunday, we celebrated Medical Worker Day. I would like to once again congratulate doctors, paramedics, ambulance crews, and junior medical personnel on their professional holiday. Special words of gratitude also go to the professors and instructors of medical universities and colleges, as well as to all practicing specialists working in clinics, hospitals, and federal medical centres who share their knowledge and experience and help train the younger generation. This continuity is undoubtedly essential for improving the quality of medical care in the country. The noble and creative tradition of senior peers supporting young medical professionals originated in the pre-revolutionary period, matured and grew stronger during the Soviet era, and in recent years we have done much to ensure that mentoring in healthcare has become an important tool for training specialists. At the end of last year, the relevant provisions were enshrined in a dedicated law. Its implementation will, in fact, begin in just a few days, when approximately 220,000 medical school graduates receive their diplomas. After completing their initial accreditation, young specialists will embark on their professional careers under the guidance of skilled mentors. I would like to emphasise that this is not a system of mandatory job assignments, like it was during the Soviet era. Graduates are free to choose a medical institution to start their career. The key requirement is that the institution is included in the programme of state guarantee of free medical care. We must not allow the mentoring programme to become a mere formality or to place an excessive burden on healthcare workers, including through an unjustified increase in bureaucratic requirements. An approach like this would invalidate the very purpose of the decisions that have been adopted. In this connection, I would like to ask the State Council Commission on Long and Active Life, the Russian Popular Front, and the National Medical Chamber to organise monitoring of the programme’s implementation at the local level. We need to maintain close contact with young specialists and their mentors, get their feedback and analyse their comments and suggestions. (After that, several participants delivered their reports on current issues. Transport Minister Andrei Nikitin pointed out that the transport sector was preparing for a major transformation, which will determine its development in the coming decades. On September 1, 2026, commercial invoices will switch to a digital format within the State Information System of Electronic Transport Documents. The system has been functioning in test mode for the past four years and now includes 14,000 companies in 83 regions. Alexei Tsydenov, Head of the Republic of Buryatia and Chairman of the State Council Commission on an Effective Transport System, shared his comments and additional points on that issue. The President emphasised
